Title:Achieving Unanimous Consensus Through Multi-Agent Deliberation

Authors:Apurba Pokharel, Ram Dantu, Shakila Zaman, Vinh Quach, Sirisha Talapuru
View a PDF of the paper titled Achieving Unanimous Consensus Through Multi-Agent Deliberation, by Apurba Pokharel and 4 other authors
View PDF HTML (experimental)
Abstract:Blockchain consensus mechanisms have relied on algorithms such as Proof-of-Work (PoW) and Proof-of-Stake (PoS) to ensure network functionality and integrity. However, these approaches struggle with adaptability for decision-making where the opinions of each matter rather than reaching an agreement based on honest majority or weighted consensus. This paper introduces a novel deliberation-based consensus mechanism where Large Language Models (LLMs) act as rational agents engaging in structured discussions to reach a unanimous consensus. By leveraging graded consensus and a multi-round deliberation process, our approach ensures unanimous consensus for definitive problems and graded consensus for prioritized decision problems and policies. We provide a formalization of our system and use it to show that the properties of blockchains are maintained, while also addressing the behavior in terms of adversaries, stalled deliberations, and confidence in consensus. Moreover, experimental results demonstrate system feasibility, showcasing convergence, block properties, and accuracy, which enable deliberative decision-making on blockchain networks.
Comments: 6 pages, 4 figure, 2 tables

Achieving Unanimous Consensus Through Multi-Agent Deliberation

Apurba Pokharel    Ram Dantu    Shakila Zaman    Vinh Quach    Sirisha Talapuru
Abstract

Blockchain consensus mechanisms have relied on algorithms such as Proof-of-Work (PoW) and Proof-of-Stake (PoS) to ensure network functionality and integrity. However, these approaches struggle with adaptability for decision-making where the opinions of each matter rather than reaching an agreement based on honest majority or weighted consensus. This paper introduces a novel deliberation-based consensus mechanism where Large Language Models (LLMs) act as rational agents engaging in structured discussions to reach a unanimous consensus. By leveraging graded consensus and a multi-round deliberation process, our approach ensures unanimous consensus for definitive problems and graded consensus for prioritized decision problems and policies. We provide a formalization of our system and use it to show that the properties of blockchains are maintained, while also addressing the behavior in terms of adversaries, stalled deliberations, and confidence in consensus. Moreover, experimental results demonstrate system feasibility, showcasing convergence, block properties, and accuracy, which enable deliberative decision-making on blockchain networks.

I Introduction and Motivation

Absence of Deliberative Consensus in Blockchain: Blockchains introduced a new way of processing financial transactions within a decentralized network, which guarantees that transactions are validated and recorded by the network through a consensus mechanism (e.g., PoW [21], PoS [36], etc. ). However, a key observation is made that the majority of the consensus protocols being used in blockchains are designed for cryptocurrencies [16] or financial applications [38], [37], [39]. Moreover, these protocols are primarily designed with an agreement quorum to handle the presence of faulty/malicious nodes. For instance, PoW considers a 51% majority, whereas PoS-BFT practices 33% adversary tolerance [15]. Although these kinds of agreements are advantageous when considering failure or malice, they cannot guarantee a reliable solution for high-stakes applications (e.g., court verdict systems, healthcare decisions, ethics committees, and government policy-making systems) that require unanimous consensus among all participants [24]. For instance, a jury should reflect a fair verdict by evaluating all committee members’ opinions through rounds of structured deliberation to prevent biased or unfair decisions [29]. In healthcare, decisions such as transitioning to human drug testing require strong or near-unanimous agreement, which is likewise impossible with current consensus mechanisms. Figure 1 represents the limitations of current consensus protocols.

Contribution of Blockchain to Deliberation: Deliberation is used prevalently in robotics [2], governance [19], and Artificial Intelligence (AI) [5], highlighting its impact on improving decision-making performance. However, current deliberation implementations are limited in structured reasoning, argument verification, decision refinements, ensuring participation, and transparency [20]. Blockchain’s key feature of transparency and immutability takes deliberative consensus to another level. Not only do these features allow full visibility and analysis of how a certain decision was made, but they also allow the decision-making process to undergo public auditing. This is an excellent choice for reviewing decisions in critical sectors such as legal, healthcare, and government [27], [28].

II Problem Definition

The key idea of implementing a consensus mechanism in a decentralized blockchain network is to establish agreement and trust toward maintaining a unified and consistent ledger without a central authority [36]. However, existing consensus algorithms are not designed to address the requirements of high-stakes applications that demand unanimous consensus with deliberation. For instance, a trial case in a courtroom requires all jurors to fully agree to decide whether someone is guilty before a verdict can be reached. With a limited agreement structure and no deliberation practice in current consensus protocols (e.g., PoW, PoS, PoS-BFT, DPoS, etc. [24]), it fails to allow enough coordination with participants. Furthermore, even though deliberation has been used to generate effective convergence in various real-life use-cases [5], [12, 1], challenges including limited argument structure, lack of proposal refinements, biased or limited knowledge-based reasoning still hinder its ability to consistently produce fair and reliable outcomes for complex networks like blockchain [31, 32].

III Contribution

To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to systematically identify the requirements of a deliberative unanimous consensus in blockchain networks for high-stakes applications, with the following contributions:

  1. 1.

    Pioneering work using a multi-agent-based deliberation framework to achieve unanimous consensus in blockchain. (See Section VI) (See Figure 4)

  2. 2.

    Formally defining the deliberative consensus protocol, and ensuring blockchain properties of consistency, agreement, and liveness hold under adversarial and stalled deliberation conditions.(See Section XI-A)

  3. 3.

    Deliberation protocol evaluated on diverse datasets mirrors real-world deliberation where knowledgeable agents converge in fewer rounds, while less-informed ones struggle. (See Section VIII, Figure 2)

  4. 4.

    The novel implementation work is open-sourced and made available [26].

IV Literature Review

IV-1 Cognitive and Social Models of Consensus

Negotiation is the process of bargaining with offers and counteroffers until an agreement is reached [4]. In Argument-based negotiation, participants exchange ”for” and ”against” arguments to negotiate [5, 25]. On the other hand, voting is a well-known method for reaching consensus [13]. The work by Barabas makes the case that deliberation differs from discussion and other forms of consensus by allowing participants to soften strongly held views, encounter different perspectives, and learn readily [3]. Deliberation’s open-ended nature and expressiveness make it a better choice than negotiation and voting.

IV-2 Consensus on Blockchain

Lamport et al. addressed the byzantine generals problem to establish a distributed system where the number of honest nodes must satisfy n>3t+1, where n is the total number of nodes and t is the number of dishonest nodes [15]. Bitcoin later introduced a novel approach to financial transactions, ensuring finality through majority agreement among honest participants [21]. Even in fully honest networks, finality depends on majority agreement. Protocols like PoS and DPoS adopt this model by selecting validators for efficient finalization [36]. This mechanism preserves system integrity while optimizing transaction processing speed to remain competitive with centralized systems. However, existing consensus models prioritize majority agreement over full participation, potentially overlooking critical perspectives. On the other hand, distributed applications such as Aragon Court [11] and Kleros [17] focus on fact-based adjudication rather than structured communication. Similarly, off-chain governance solutions like Decred’s Politeia [10] and Snapshot [23] allow stakeholders to vote but lack structured deliberation. Moreover, reliance on off-chain discussions introduces transparency and security concerns, as critical decision-making steps occur outside the blockchain.

IV-3 LLMs and Deliberations

LLMs exhibit strong reasoning and generation abilities, enabling tasks like summarization, debate, and reflection with high accuracy [18]. Techniques such as Chain of Thought [34] decompose complex tasks into subtasks, inspiring collaborative approaches where multiple LLMs interact to enhance reasoning [12]. These include cooperative [9], adversarial [7], or role-based settings [33]. However, these methods prioritize final outputs, discarding the deliberation process and thus lacking auditability and transparency. An in-depth overview of Multi-agent LLM systems is also presented in [18].

V Prompt Engineering for LLM Optimization

The utterance from an LLM greatly depends on the nature of the prompt given to it. To this end we use two kinds of prompting techniques defined below.

V-1 Initial Prompting

The initial prompting phase (Algorithm 1) gathers each model’s (L​L​MNLLM_{N}) response rNr_{N} to the problem P​rPr, storing them in the response set R0R^{0} for deliberation. To enhance realism and mimic real-world deliberation, we use a mix of Chain of Thought (CoT) prompts (PC​o​TP_{CoT}) and Zero Shot (ZS) prompts (PZ​SP_{ZS}). CoT prompting improves response quality over ZS prompting [34].

V-2 Iterative Prompting/ Prompt Chaining

In order to allow change of opinions to take place the models are prompted in iterations (Algorithm 2) where each iteration is defined as turns TT. In each turn tt, each agent receives its own utterance from the previous turn ii as Rit−1R_{i}^{t-1} and the previous turn’s neighbor agent’s responses as Rt−1R^{t-1} as context and is tasked with evaluating and improving its own answer.

VI Deliberation Framework

To mimic real deliberation, the process unfolds over multiple rounds, as shown in Figure 4. We make an assumption that a solvable problem is selected for deliberation.

VI-1 Initial Round

For the initial round, initial prompting (Algorithm 1) is used to collect individual perspectives from each model. Each utterance (rN)(r_{N}) is stored in the response set R0R^{0} and gossiped across the network. The number of agents (NN) shapes the diversity of perspectives but increases storage overhead and prolongs convergence (see VIII-B, Table II).

VI-2 Reflection Round

For the second round, prompt chaining (Algorithm 2) allows agents to iteratively refine their responses over TT turns. Mimicking real-world deliberation, the process begins with diverse opinions and moves toward consensus. If unanimous agreement is reached, the round proceeds; otherwise, if convergence fails within TT turns, deliberation is declared hung and terminated. A round-robin strategy governs speaking order.

VI-3 Conclusion Round

For the final round of deliberation, a block is mined based on the outcome of the reflection round. If a unanimous consensus is reached, the full deliberation history is stored on-chain; otherwise, an empty block is mined to indicate a hung deliberation.

VII Integrating Deliberation in Blockchain

This section describes how the underlying blockchain protocol is extended to support LLM-based multi-agent deliberation to generate a unanimous consensus.

VII-1 Integrating LLMs in Blockchains

LLMs are a part of the blockchain node. Each node will be running its own LLM model and will call the model with prompts as mentioned in algorithm 1 and 2. Once the model replies, the utterance is propagated to every node in the network via a gossip protocol.

VII-2 Consistency, Authenticity, and Propagation of Utterance

Utterances are signed by the producing agent and propagated via a two-phase gossip protocol. In phase one, nodes broadcast hashes of utterances; in phase two, peers request full data if needed. This reduces bandwidth while maintaining authenticity and consistency, as signatures are verifiable.

VII-3 Storage, Verification and Extension of Ledger

Once consensus is reached, the complete deliberation is stored on-chain to ensure transparency and enable future dispute resolution. The resulting block is propagated across the network, allowing each model to verify its contents for integrity. Upon successful verification, the block is appended to the ledger and gossiped further. If consensus is not achieved within the predefined time 𝒯\mathcal{T}, an empty block is mined, following the principle that ”failure to agree is agreement to defer” [30].

VIII Experimental Results

Implementation Detail: This proof of concept extends the PoW client from Nimiq Blockchain[35] to build the proposed deliberation protocol. We use LLMs (Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct and Llama-2-13b-chat-hf) with NVIDIA RTX 6000 Ada Generation GPU. The GitHub repository is public [26]. The different dataset used are shown in table I.

VIII-A Impact of Deliberation

Time to Reach Consensus (TRC): Time taken to reach a unanimous consensus when varying agents, and turns are represented in Table II. Table shows that the prompt generation time (PGT) is the primary contributor to increasing TRC. Also, the number of agents and the number of turns directly correlate to more time due to more discussion taking place.

Relation Of Accuracy With Turns: Figure 2 shows consensus accuracy (correct agents / total) evolving through deliberation. Deliberating on the same problem, models with fewer parameters have low convergence and accuracy, whereas larger model achieve higher accuracy and convergence.

Comparison With Other Deliberation Protocols: Figure 3(a) compares the accuracy of our protocol (80%) with results reported in [9] and [7] on the GSM8K dataset.

VIII-B Blockchain Performance

Analysis of Block Size: Figure 3(b) shows the blocksize obtained at the end of deliberation on the GSM8k dataset. The theoretical max size of our block is 2322^{32} bytes. However, the size is limited to 100 kb.

Comparison with other Blockchain protocols: A direct comparison is not feasible because block production time varies in our deliberative approach. During experiments, maximum block production took up to 150 seconds, with block sizes staying under 30KB. Current consensus approach require an honest majority; however, our deliberative protocol requires unanimity. Figure 3(c) demonstrates this relationship.

IX Conclusion and Future Work

This paper proposes a novel paradigm that integrates large language models (LLMs) within a blockchain to achieve unanimous consensus through deliberation. Our literature review highlights limitations in existing consensus protocols for decision-making and identifies gaps in the emerging field of LLM-driven blockchain consensus. To address this, we present a formal representation of using multi-agents to achieve unanimous consensus in decision-making through deliberation. Performance analysis on the proof of concept implementation work has also been presented. However, the system encounters potential challenges such as LLM reliability (hallucinations), security risks (bias, adversarial prompts), scalability (GPU and power demands), and Sybil attacks. These can be mitigated with validation, incentive mechanisms, efficient fine-tuning and off-chain storage, and reputation-based defenses respectively. Future work will address the scalability of the deliberation framework, as well as explore prioritized deliberation more robustly.

X Acknowledgment

This research was partially supported by NSA grants H98230-20-1-0329, H98230-20-1-0414, H98230-21-1-0262, and H98230-22-1-0329. We thank Dr. Pascal Berrang from Nimiq, who helped us out initially with Nimiq’s codebase.

XI Appendix

XI-A Modeling Unanimous Consensus

Deliberation Game: In order to prove the correctness of our deliberation framework, this section models our system as a cooperative game in which agents provide arguments that can be countered, refined, or enhanced. The end goal of this game is to arrive at a unanimous consensus through multiple iterations of the game rounds.

Definition XI.1.

Let DD be nn player game where AnA_{n} represents the nth player, work together to solve a problem PP by performing valid actions A​CrAC_{r} in each iterative round rr . The goal of the game is to achieve unanimous consensus with confidence CC within a timebound 𝒯\mathcal{T} with high confidence and the players receive a payoff P​A​YPAY based on their contribution.
Where, Di=(P,An,A​Cr,P​A​Y,C,t)D^{i}=(P,A_{n},AC_{r},PAY,C,t), DiD^{i} is the it​hi^{th} deliberation done in the network, stored in a block BB and tt is the time at which the deliberation completes.

XI-B Start of deliberation

Definition XI.2.

The deliberation game DD is said to be initiated if it meets one of the following criteria:

  1. 1.

    The problem PP hasn’t been deliberated before.

  2. 2.

    If deliberation was hung before P∈HgP\in H_{g}. Then Ank!=AniA_{n}^{k}!=A_{n}^{i}, where ii is the current deliberation, AnkA_{n}^{k} is the set of hung deliberators, and ,Ani,A_{n}^{i} is the new set of deliberators.

XI-C Unanimous Consensus Criteria

The type of consensus reached depends on the nature of the problem being deliberated on. Definitive problems have unique solutions, whereas problems involving prioritized preferences do not, giving rise to the notion of graded consensus [6].

Definition XI.3.

In a definitive deliberative game, DD is in unanimous consensus if, for every n agents with m malicious agents, each honest agent hh outputs a value-argument pair A​Cri=(Vri,A​r​gri)AC_{r}^{i}=(V_{r}^{i},Arg_{r}^{i}) at the end of round rr where the value is the answer to the problems along with arguments that support it, so that ∀i,j∈h:Vri=Vrj\forall i,j\in h:\quad V_{r}^{i}=V_{r}^{j}, C=1C=1 and t<𝒯t<\mathcal{T}.

Definition XI.4.

In a prioritized deliberative game, we say that DD is in unanimous consensus if, for every n agents with m malicious agents, each honest agent hh outputs a policy A​Cri={P​o1i,P​o2i,…​P​oxi}AC_{r}^{i}=\{Po_{1}^{i},Po_{2}^{i},...Po_{x}^{i}\} at the end of round rr where P​okPo_{k} are the policies proposed and are relevant to problem PP, so that θ\theta is the fraction of honest agents that include policy P​okPo_{k} in their action set and t<𝒯t<\mathcal{T}.

Definition XI.5.

For a prioritized deliberation game, the confidence in the achieved consensus is measured as follows: Let A​Cri={P​o1i,P​o2i,…,P​oxi}AC_{r}^{i}=\{Po_{1}^{i},Po_{2}^{i},\dots,Po_{x}^{i}\} be the set of policies proposed by agent ii at the end of round rr.

For each unique policy P​okPo_{k}, its agreement level is defined as:

A​(P​ok)=∑i∈h𝟙​(P​ok∈A​Cri)|h|A(Po_{k})=\frac{\sum_{i\in h}\mathbb{1}(Po_{k}\in AC_{r}^{i})}{|h|}

where 𝟙​(P​ok∈A​Cri)\mathbb{1}(Po_{k}\in AC_{r}^{i}) is 1 if agent ii included P​okPo_{k} in A​CriAC_{r}^{i}, and 0 otherwise. If the agreement level exceeds threshold θ\theta, the policy is added to the accepted policy set:

A​(P​ok)≥θ,A​c​c​(P​o)=A​c​c​(P​o)∪A​(P​ok)A(Po_{k})\geq\theta,\quad Acc(Po)=Acc(Po)\cup A(Po_{k})

The overall confidence in consensus is then computed as:

T=len​(A​c​c​(P​o)),C=∑A​(P​oa)T∀A​(P​oa)∈A​c​c​(P​o)T=\text{len}(Acc(Po)),\quad C=\frac{\sum A(Po_{a})}{T}\quad\forall A(Po_{a})\in Acc(Po)

where CC represents the confidence in consensus.

XI-D Termination of deliberation

Definition XI.6.

The deliberation is said to be successfully terminated if the criteria for unanimous consensus are achieved. The deliberation parameters DiD^{i} are stored on the chain, and a new block is created.

Definition XI.7.

The deliberation is said to be in hung deliberation if: t>𝒯t>\mathcal{T} or if the number of honest participants falls below a threshold. The deliberation is then terminated by adding problem PP to the hung problem set HgH_{g}: Hg=Hg∪PH_{g}=H_{g}\cup P and producing an empty block along with the deliberation parameters DiD^{i}.

XI-E System Properties

Theorem XI.1.

Proof of consistency.

XI-E1 For definitive problem

For a definitive problem PP, initially honest player(s) l∈hl\in h will output a value-argument pair (Vr,A​r​gr)(V_{r},Arg_{r}) at the end of round rr such that (Vr)(V_{r}) is same for all players ll in the group.
After r1,r1>rr_{1},r_{1}>r rounds of deliberation all honest player i,j∈hi,j\in h and l<j,il<j,i outputs a value-argument pair (Vr1,A​r​gr1)(V_{r_{1}},Arg_{r_{1}}) such that ∀i,j∈h\forall i,j\in h: Vr1i=Vr1jV_{r_{1}}^{i}=V_{r_{1}}^{j}. This implies that every honest player will output the same value ensuring consistency.

XI-E2 For Prioritized Problems

For a prioritized problem PP, initially, each honest agent i∈hi\in h outputs a set of proposed policies at the end of round rr: A​Cri={P​o1i,P​o2i,…,P​oxi}AC_{r}^{i}=\{Po_{1}^{i},Po_{2}^{i},...,Po_{x}^{i}\} where P​okiPo_{k}^{i} represents a policy proposed by agent ii at the end of round rr. The confidence C1C_{1} in consensus is low.

After r1,r1>rr_{1},r_{1}>r rounds of deliberation, every honest agent i,j∈hi,j\in h outputs a refined set of policies: A​Cr1i={P​o1r1,P​o2r1,…,P​oTr1}AC_{r_{1}}^{i}=\{Po_{1}^{r_{1}},Po_{2}^{r_{1}},...,Po_{T}^{r_{1}}\} such that the confidence C2C_{2} in consensus is higher than before C2>C1C_{2}>C_{1}. This only occurs when the policies are selected consistently by more players at the end of the round r1r_{1}. Thus, for policies that are accepted and added to A​c​c​(P​o)Acc(Po), the consistency property is maintained.

Corollary XI.1.1.

Proof of agreement.
The consistency property goes hand in hand with agreement. There can be no consistency without agreeing on the same values/policies, and no agreement without consistent outputs.

Theorem XI.2.

Proof of liveness
Based on the definitions XI.6 and XI.7, it can be stated that block production continues for both cases, ensuring liveness of the network.
